channel.title: NYT > Science
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Why Falling Cats Always Seem to Land on Their Feet
item.description: It takes backbone to solve an enigma like the “falling cat” problem.
item.dc:creator: Taylor Mitchell Brown

item.title: Slowly, Slowly, ‘Darwin’s Finches of the Snail World’ Return From Near Extinction
item.description: Partula snails all but vanished from Polynesia after the arrival of a carnivorous foreign snail. But a global alliance of zoos has worked to bring them back.
item.dc:creator: Franz Lidz

item.title: Bumblebee Queens Can Breathe Underwater
item.description: A new study offers clues as to how the insects survive flooding as they emerge from a hibernation-like phase every winter.
item.dc:creator: Jason P. Dinh

item.title: In Ancient Peru, a Parrot Trade That Crossed the Andes
item.description: Scientists studied centuries-old bird feathers from an ancient tomb on the coast, and then traced the origins back to the Amazon.
item.dc:creator: Alexa Robles-Gil
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: The researchers identified four distinct parrot species originating from the Amazon: the blue-and-yellow macaw, pictured; the scarlet macaw; the red-and-green macaw; and the mealy Amazon.

item.title: Meteorite Crashes Through Roof in Germany After Fiery Light Show
item.description: The fireball from space was spotted by a network of sky-watching cameras in Belgium, France, Luxembourg, the Netherlands and Germany.
item.dc:creator: Robin George Andrews

item.title: Viral Outbreaks Take a Common Path from Animals to People, Study Finds
item.description: Researchers have devised a new tool for discerning between naturally occurring viral outbreaks and those resulting from lab accidents.
item.dc:creator: Carl Zimmer
item.media:credit: Ruth Fremson/The New York Times
item.media:description: A family physician testing patients for the Covid-19 virus at a clinic in Oregon in May 2020.

item.title: ‘Don’t Worry About It’: NASA Satellite to Burn Up in Fall to Earth
item.description: Most of the Van Allen Probe A, in orbit since 2012, will incinerate in Earth’s atmosphere, NASA said. But some pieces are expected to survive, posing a small risk to people on the ground.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.media:credit: NASA
item.media:description: An artist’s rendering of the Van Allen Probe satellites in orbit. One of the satellites, the Van Allen Probe A, was expected to re-enter Earth’s atmosphere on Tuesday.

item.title: MoMath Brings Prime Numbers to a Prime New Location
item.description: After outgrowing its original home, the National Museum of Mathematics has added new exhibits and an art gallery space in what was an empty storefront along the Avenue of the Americas in Manhattan.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang and Emon Hassan

item.title: A Night Light in the Sky? Reflect Orbital Wants to Launch a Big Space Mirror.
item.description: The company is seeking F.C.C. approval to test an idea to reflect sunlight to Earth at night, possibly powering solar panels. Critics say it could be bad for people and wildlife.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang and Hiroko Tabuchi
item.media:credit: Tim Gruber for The New York Times
item.media:description: The start-up Reflect Orbital wants to launch mirror-bearing satellites which it says could address one of the biggest weaknesses of solar power: that electric generation stops when the sun goes down.

item.title: A Third of Americans Have Cut Spending or Borrowed Money for Health Care
item.description: As medical costs rise, more than 80 million people have made sacrifices like skipping meals and driving less, a new survey finds.
item.dc:creator: Reed Abelson
item.media:credit: Arin Yoon/Reuters
item.media:description: A recent poll found that health care costs topped a list of the public’s economic anxieties, above concerns about the prices of food and groceries, gas and utilities.

item.title: Anthony J. Leggett Dies at 87; Won Nobel for Theories on Superfluids
item.description: When scientists unwittingly turned helium into a superfluid — a feat many thought was impossible — Dr. Leggett not only recognized what had happened but also explained how.
item.dc:creator: Dylan Loeb McClain

item.title: Iran Shocks Could Spur a Shift to Clean Energy — But Also to Coal
item.description: Environmentalists say the war’s effects on oil and gas prices are an argument for countries to get off fossil fuels. But the reality is likely to be messier.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer and Lisa Friedman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Majid Saeedi/Getty Images
item.media:description: A plume of smoke from Israeli-U. S. airstrikes on Tehran on March 2.

item.title: In Talking to Parents About Vaccines, Pediatricians Navigate a Sea of Misinformation
item.description: Practitioners nationwide are striving to do what’s best for children’s health, while staying supportive in the face of mistrust and confusion.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli and Maddie McGarvey
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times

item.title: Cancer Haunts Neighbors of Canada’s Oil Sands Wastelands
item.description: Though high rates of the disease persist among the nearby Indigenous communities, the Canadian government is weighing rules that may allow energy giants to release treated mining waste into the river system.
item.dc:creator: Emily Baumgaertner Nunn
item.media:credit: Pat Kane for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Syncrude facility near Fort McMurray, Alberta.

item.title: Oil Prices Will Remain High as Iran War Continues
item.description: While officials look for ways to ease oil shocks, experts say higher prices will likely persist until traffic through the Strait of Hormuz returns.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Black smoke filled the skies above Tehran on Sunday morning after Israeli airstrikes struck energy facilities overnight.

item.title: F.D.A. Opens Door to More Flavored E-Cigarettes
item.description: The agency is proposing allowing other tastes for vapes, like mint, coffees, spices or tea, to appeal to adults. At the same time, illicit candy-flavored types aimed at teenagers have flooded the market.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The F.D.A. said it would consider vapes in flavors such as mint, coffees, teas and spices like cinnamon, but would continue to reject those offering sweet or fruity flavors.

item.title: Lemurs Love This Fruit That Is Choking Madagascar’s Forests
item.description: The strawberry guava, one of the world’s worst invasive species, hinders forest restoration on the island while feeding its famous endangered primates.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Nuwer

item.title: For These Design Materials, It’s Goodbye and Good Riddance
item.description: Not all acts of extinction are to be regretted.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Treffinger
item.media:credit: Armando Babani/European Pressphoto Agency
item.media:description: PFAS, so-called forever chemicals, were developed in the early 20th century, and are the reason Teflon cookware doesn’t stick, why many pizza boxes resist grease and why clothing can be waterproof.

item.title: Divisive F.D.A. Vaccine Regulator Is Resigning
item.description: Dr. Vinay Prasad drew criticism for overriding career scientists and rejecting drugs by companies seeking agency approval.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett
item.media:credit: U.S. Food and Drug Administration
item.media:description: Dr. Vinay Prasad’s refusal to accept Moderna’s application for a new mRNA flu vaccine, prompting an uproar among companies and experts.

item.title: NASA’s Asteroid-Smashing DART Mission Sped Up Space Rock’s Orbit Around the Sun
item.description: New data about the DART spacecraft’s effects adds evidence that Earth could be defended from future deadly asteroids by diverting their orbits.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:credit: NASA/Johns Hopkins APL
item.media:description: Dimorphos is about 525 feet around and orbits a larger parent asteroid, Didymos.

item.title: From 1968: Lise Meitner, Physicist, Is Dead at 89; Paved Way for Splitting of Atom
item.description: She laid much of the theoretical groundwork for the atomic bomb, although she did not participate directly in its production.
item.dc:creator: Special to The New York Times
item.media:description: Lise Meitner in an undated photo with the German nuclear chemist Otto Hahn.

item.title: From 2000: Hedy Lamarr, Sultry Star Who Reigned in Hollywood
item.description: A temptress on the silver screen in the 1930s and ’40s, she later became an inventor.
item.dc:creator: Richard Severo
item.media:credit: Clarence Sinclair Bull/Hulton Archive, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Hedy Lamarr posing for a promotional portrait for the 1944 film “The Heavenly Body.”

item.title: From Endangered Plants Come Whiffs of Inspiration
item.description: How scientists, conservationists and the fragrance industry are working to revive extinct scents and perpetuate threatened ones.
item.dc:creator: Tammy LaGorce

item.title: Superfood Fuels Mating Frenzy for Critically Endangered Kakapo
item.description: Kakapos, which are reclusive and flightless and can live as long as humans, are found only in New Zealand. They feed on the fruit of the rimu tree.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.media:credit: Andrew Digby/Dept. of Conservation, New Zealand, via Associated Press
item.media:description: A kakapo with eggs on Anchor Island, in New Zealand. The island is known for lacking the kinds of predators that threaten the parrots.

item.title: Fewer Pregnant Women Received Acetaminophen in E.R.s After White House Warnings
item.description: A new analysis of prescription data found that emergency room orders for acetaminophen for pregnant women fell for weeks after the federal warning.
item.dc:creator: Azeen Ghorayshi
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump made an announcement about autism and Tylenol alongside Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. in the Roosevelt Room of the White House in Washington last September.

item.title: Feeling the Effects of 260,000 Federal Jobs Lost
item.description: One year in, assessing budget cuts to federal climate and science jobs.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown
item.media:credit: Chip Somodevilla/Getty Images
item.media:description: Demonstrators gathered to protest against budget cuts outside the headquarters of the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration in Silver Spring, Maryland, last year.

item.title: Nature Report, Killed by Trump, Is Released Independently
item.description: A draft assessment of the health of nature in the United States is grim but shot through with bright spots and possibility.
item.dc:creator: Catrin Einhorn
item.category: National Academies of Sciences, Engineering and Medicine
item.media:credit: David Robert Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: A dried part of the Arkansas River in Garden City, Kan.

item.title: Should New York City Burn Its Parks? This Scientist Thinks So.
item.description: An unusual outbreak of wildfires in city parks gave scientists a chance to study these rare events. Now they’re coming to different conclusions.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey and Karsten Moran
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times

item.title: You’ve Never Seen Ants Like This Before
item.description: The Antscan database captures the tiny insects’ diversity and shows how the world’s living things could be rendered in three dimensions.
item.dc:creator: Ari Daniel

item.title: F.D.A. Faces Upset Over Denials of New Drugs
item.description: Agency officials promise fast reviews of new treatments while vowing they will not be a “rubber stamp” for the industry. But patients with rare diseases view recent decisions as signs that the doors are closing on their options.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett
item.media:credit: Will Crooks for The New York Times
